text: Scientific literature contains a wealth of knowledge about the structures and properties of materials. This knowledge can be extracted from scientific texts to accelerate the search for novel materials. However, conventional approaches in natural language processing (NLP) such as named entity recognition (NER) are not suitable to express the sophisticated, hierarchical relationships among the entities in scientific materials, for instance, the relationship between the chemistry of a material, its structure and its behavior. In this study, we present a flexible, approach to realize joint NER and relation extraction (RE) by fine-tuning pre-trained large language models (LLMs) such as GPT-3 and Llama-2. The LLMs can be trained with only about 100–600 instances of annotated text to extract advanced scientific contents from scientific texts and output them to structured formats such as JSON or other suitable formats in natural language. This approach was validated on three materials chemistry tasks, i.e., host dopant relationship extraction, metal organic frameworks (MOFs) extraction and general materials information extraction. It has been shown that the LLMs have better performance in extracting complex relations than the BERT-based models that are widely used in scientific information extraction. In addition, the LLMs benefit from the built-in entity normalization and post edit correction functionalities. We have also demonstrated that the partially pre-trained LLMs can be used in the human-in-the-loop mode to reduce the annotation time for the data scientists by over 50%. This approach not only facilitates the building of high-accuracy materials databases based on scientific literature but also requires minimal expertise in NLP.
